questions: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' originally authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey. Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license linked in the tutorial's footer. According to the official legal deed for that specific license version, what are the two core freedoms listed under 'You are free to', the three conditions listed under 'Under the following terms', and what specific newer version number does the license page explicitly recommend as being 'more internationally robust'?

rounds.question: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' originally authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey. Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license linked in the tutorial's footer. According to the official legal deed for that specific license version, what are the two core freedoms listed under 'You are free to', the three conditions listed under 'Under the following terms', and what specific newer version number does the license page explicitly recommend as being 'more internationally robust'?
checklist_width: Freedom 1: Share (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format)
Freedom 2: Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Condition 2: NonCommercial (may not use for commercial purposes)
Condition 3: ShareAlike (must distribute contributions under the same license)
checklist_depth: Identified License: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0)
Logic Proof: The agent connected M. Stonebank's specific tutorial to the legacy 2.0 license link found on the page.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Reasoning to link a specific legacy tutorial (M. Stonebank's) to its precise license version (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0), which is not explicitly named in the query. It then requires Wide Aggregation to retrieve specific clauses (Freedoms/Terms) and a unique 'Notice' detail (the recommendation of version 4.0) found only on the deed page for that specific older license.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The original M. Stonebank “UNIX Tutorial for Beginners” from the University of Surrey is preserved on the university’s digital archives, with its footer prominently linking to the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK) license [1]. Per the license’s official legal deed, the two core freedoms under “You are free to” are: 1) copy, distribute, display, and publicly perform the work; 2) create derivative works based on the original [2]. The three conditions under “Under the following terms” are: 1) attribution (clearly credit the original author and include full license details); 2) non-commercial use (prohibit any commercial exploitation of the work or its derivatives); 3) share alike (any derivative works must be licensed under the same CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK terms [2]. The official license page explicitly identifies version 4.0 as the more internationally robust newer version, noting its enhanced cross-jurisdictional compatibility and clearer terms for global use cases [2]. This preserved tutorial remains accessible for non-commercial educational use, aligning with the license’s constraints and the author’s original intent for beginner-focused UNIX education [1].

## References
[1] University of Surrey. (n.d.). UNIX Tutorial for Beginners by M. Stonebank. Retrieved from https://www.surrey.ac.uk/computing/students/tutorials/unix-tutorial
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Legal Deed: Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/legalcode
rounds.answer_b:
The "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey can be found at the University of Surrey's website. The footer of the tutorial indicates that it is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales license [1].

The official legal deed for this license version lists two core freedoms under "You are free to":
1. Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format
2. Adapt — remix, transform, and build upon the material [2]

The three conditions listed under "Under the following terms" are:
1. Attribution — You must give appropriate credit, provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made
2. NonCommercial — You may not use the material for commercial purposes
3. ShareAlike — If you remix, transform, or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under the same license as the original [2]

The license page explicitly recommends version 4.0 as being "more internationally robust" [3].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (n.d.). UNIX Tutorial for Beginners. University of Surrey. Retrieved from https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/teaching/unix/
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK). Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 deed. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B followed the formatting instructions perfectly, using bullet points to list the freedoms and conditions, making it highly readable. Agent B also correctly extracted the exact keywords ('Share' and 'Adapt') from the Legal Deed. Agent A provided a 'wall of text' which is difficult to scan and used descriptive definitions rather than the specific terms listed on the deed. Agent B provided a better user experience.
